label: Clock-synchronized multi-die programmable logic
explanation: Documents describe integrated circuits with programmable logic fabric partitioned across dies using chip-to-chip interconnects and interface bridges that exchange data synchronized to a transmitted clock (e.g., input buffering and clock synchronization units). Top words like circuit, programmable, die, clock, integrated, and noc reflect an SoC-level focus on clocked inter-die communication and programmable-logic configuration/acceleration.
candidate_labels: Clocked interfaces for multi-die programmable ICs
Programmable logic die interconnect and clocking
candidate_explanations: Documents describe integrated circuits with programmable logic fabric partitioned across dies using chip-to-chip interconnects and interface bridges that exchange data synchronized to a transmitted clock (e.g., input buffering and clock synchronization units). Top words like circuit, programmable, die, clock, integrated, and noc reflect an SoC-level focus on clocked inter-die communication and programmable-logic configuration/acceleration.

Top words (circuit, clock, programmable, die, soc, noc) and the example claims describe clock-edge buffering and synchronization, CPU/PLD role partitioning for programmable logic configuration, and silicon-bridge chip-to-chip source-synchronous interfaces. The unifying technical theme is design of clocked, synchronized interconnects and interface circuitry for multi-die programmable integrated systems (SoC/PLD).

Top words (circuit, logic, chip, clock, programmable, die, soc) indicate IC-level design. The documents describe programmable logic fabrics and MP/PLD co-processing, clock synchronization and input buffering, and silicon-bridge chip-to-chip interfaces with source‑synchronous bridges. Together they point to design of inter-die programmable-logic systems and their clocked interface circuitry.

label: Content-aware seam carving
explanation: Top words (pixel, energy, seam, region, luminance, resolution) and the example documents describe computing per-pixel energy maps, identifying lowest-cost seams (including via lower-resolution acceleration structures), and removing or inserting seams. The methods perform seam-based, region-specific image resizing to preserve important pixels and image content.
candidate_explanations: Top words (pixel, energy, seam, region, luminance, resolution) and the example documents describe computing per-pixel energy maps, identifying lowest-cost seams (including via lower-resolution acceleration structures), and removing or inserting seams. The methods perform seam-based, region-specific image resizing to preserve important pixels and image content.

Top words (pixel, region, luminance, edge, intensity) and the documents describe computing per-pixel energy/importance and using that to identify low-cost seams or regions. The claims cover removing or inserting seams, per-region resizing, and low-resolution acceleration structures to perform content-aware (seam carving) image resizing.

Top words (pixel, energy, seam, region, luminance, edge) and the documents describe computing per-pixel energy/importance and identifying lowest-cost seams. The claims perform removing/inserting seams or region-wise resizing (including multi-resolution acceleration) to achieve content-aware image resizing.

Representative patents discuss temperature-inversion VFT-based voltage adjustments, per-module SOC voltage scaling based on usage/battery, and fan control tied to power/temperature, which together describe thermal-aware voltage and power management techniques.

The top words (voltage, temperature, power, supply, cpu/gpu, management) and documents describe adjusting supply voltage and power states based on VFT (voltage–frequency–temperature) data, module usage and battery life, and controlling cooling via power+temperature thresholds. Focuses on dynamically adjusting supply voltages and power/performance states based on temperature (including temperature-inversion VFT), module usage and battery indications, and measured power levels. Top words (authentication, hash, encryption, MAC, signature, HSM) and documents describe nonce-based and public/private key exchanges, hash/MAC comparisons, and HSM-backed key operations. Top words (hash, encrypted, signature, MAC, key, integrity) highlight cryptographic primitives for authenticating messages and preserving integrity. The documents describe mutual authentication flows using public/private keys, nonces, HSM-stored secrets, and generation/verification of hashes and MACs to establish session keys and verify identities.

Top words like algorithm, monitoring, metrics, optimization, and action indicate an emphasis on automated analysis and performance-driven selection of mitigation strategies. Together these point to an adaptive, metric-guided system for choosing vulnerability mitigations.

Representative documents describe platforms, agents, and an SDK that identify device vulnerabilities/attacks and present or deploy diverse mitigation actions (intrusion prevention, firewall, patches). Top words such as action, metric, monitoring, optimization, and analysis reflect evaluating and selecting mitigation strategies to optimize performance and reduce false positives, matching an automated mitigation-selection design focus.

Representative documents describe systems that identify device/OS vulnerabilities and select or deploy diverse mitigation actions (e.g., intrusion prevention, firewall) via agents, platforms, and APIs to reduce false positives. Top words such as "action", "metric", "analysis", "optimization", and "monitoring" indicate a focus on orchestrating, evaluating, and optimizing mitigation strategies rather than just detection.

Representative claims describe a bridge converting PLx streams to a multiport I/O controller with transmit/receive FIFOs and DMA, multi-rate MAC lane selection, and a PHY block that buffers data and wakes the controller to transfer it. The theme is hardware for MAC/PHY interface bridging and controlled data transfer across bus/link protocols.

Top words (controller, bus, transmission, protocol, link) and the documents describe bridging between network processors and multiport I/O controllers, multi-rate MAC interfaces, transmit/receive FIFOs and DMA-driven arbiters. Covers interfaces and bridge controllers that mediate MAC and PHY layers and host buses, including transmit/receive FIFOs, DMA/arbiter logic, and multi-rate MAC mode selection. Representative documents describe PLx-to-I/O controller bridging, shared-lane MAC modes, and PHY-layer buffering/wakeup to the controller, matching top words like controller, bus, protocol, transmit, link, and commands.
